perturbation.original_text: Covenantor shall not at any time within the period commencing on the date hereof and ending on the fourth (4/th/) anniversary of the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), have any -2- <PAGE> ownership interest (of record or beneficial) in or have any interest as an employee, salesman, consultant, officer or director in, or otherwise aid or assist in any manner, any firm, corporation, limited liability company, partnership, proprietorship or other business that engages in any California county, or in any other state, country or geographic area, listed in Exhibit "A" hereof in a business that is similar to and directly or indirectly offers products or services in competition with the business in which the Company was engaged in such California county, state, country or other geographic area as of the Closing, so long as Buyer, or any successor in interest of Buyer to the business and goodwill of the Company, remains engaged in such business in any such California county, state, country or other geographic area or continues to solicit customers or potential customers therein; provided, however, that Covenantor may own, directly or indirectly, solely as an investment, the securities of any person which are traded on any national securities exchange if Covenantor (i) is not a controlling person of, or a member of a group which controls, such person or (ii) does not, directly or indirectly own two percent (2%) or more of any class of securities of such person.
perturbation.changed_text: Covenantor shall endeavor, within the period commencing on the date hereof and ending on the fourth (4/th/) anniversary of the date hereof (the "Restricted Period"), to avoid having any -2- <PAGE> ownership interest (of record or beneficial) in or have any interest as an employee, salesman, consultant, officer or director in, or otherwise aid or assist in any manner, any firm, corporation, limited liability company, partnership, proprietorship or other business that engages in any California county, or in any other state, country or geographic area, listed in Exhibit "A" hereof in a business that is similar to and directly or indirectly offers products or services in competition with the business in which the Company was engaged in such California county, state, country or other geographic area as of the Closing, so long as Buyer, or any successor in interest of Buyer to the business and goodwill of the Company, remains engaged in such business in any such California county, state, country or other geographic area or continues to solicit customers or potential customers therein; provided, however, that Covenantor may own, directly or indirectly, solely as an investment, the securities of any person which are traded on any national securities exchange if Covenantor (i) is not a controlling person of, or a member of a group which controls, such person or (ii) does not, directly or indirectly own two percent (2%) or more of any class of securities of such person.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall not' to 'shall endeavor to avoid' introduces ambiguity and weakens the non-compete obligation, making it less enforceable. 'Endeavor to avoid' is subjective and doesn't clearly prohibit competitive activities.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that '...every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.' By changing the language to 'shall endeavor to avoid,' the contract attempts to restrain competition but in a way that is ambiguous and potentially unenforceable, directly contradicting the intent of Section 16600 which disallows restraints on trade.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract used the term 'shall not' which creates a definitive restriction on the covenantor. The changed text replaces 'shall not' with 'shall endeavor to avoid,' weakening the restriction. California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that every contract that restrains someone from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is void. The change to 'shall endeavor to avoid' introduces ambiguity and weakens the enforceability of the non-compete clause, making it likely to be considered a restraint on trade that is unenforceable under BPC 16600(a), which states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void.'

perturbation.original_text: During the Restricted Period, Covenantor shall not solicit or assist any other person to solicit any business (other than for Buyer) from any present or past customer of the Company; or request or advise any present or future customer of the Company to withdraw, curtail or cancel its business dealings with Buyer; or commit any other act or assist others to commit any other act which might injure the business of Buyer.
perturbation.changed_text: During the Restricted Period, Covenantor should generally refrain from soliciting or assisting any other person to solicit any business (other than for Buyer) from any present or past customer of the Company; or request or advise any present or future customer of the Company to withdraw, curtail or cancel its business dealings with Buyer; or commit any other act or assist others to commit any other act which might injure the business of Buyer.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'shall not' with 'should generally refrain from' weakens the prohibition on soliciting customers. The word 'generally' introduces ambiguity, making it unclear when solicitation is permissible.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: This weakens the restriction against soliciting clients, which is typically included in non-compete agreements. While Section 16600 generally prohibits non-competes, narrowly tailored restrictions on soliciting existing clients can be enforceable. By making the restriction ambiguous, the contract risks being interpreted as an unenforceable restraint on trade under Section 16600.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'shall not' to 'should generally refrain from' weakens the restriction against soliciting clients. Scraped Law Snippet 1, BPC 16600(a), states that 'every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void,' with exceptions. The change introduces ambiguity that could be interpreted as an unenforceable restraint on trade, contradicting the intent of 16600(a) by creating uncertainty about the non-solicitation agreement's enforceability. While the original text was a clear prohibition, the modified text creates a potential loophole allowing for solicitation, therefore creating a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the termination of Covenantor's employment with Buyer, Covenantor shall immediately surrender to Buyer all notes, data, sketches, drawings, manuals, documents, records, data bases, programs, blueprints, memoranda, specifications, customer lists, financial reports, equipment and all other physical forms of expression incorporating or containing any Confidential Information, it being distinctly understood that all such writings, physical forms of expression and other things are the exclusive property of Buyer. Covenantor acknowledges that the unauthorized taking of any of Buyer's trade secrets is a crime under California Penal Code section 499(c) and is punishable by imprisonment in a state prison or in a county jail for a time not exceeding one year, or by a fine not exceeding five thousand dollars ($5,000), or by both such fine and such imprisonment. Covenantor further acknowledges that such unauthorized taking of Buyer's trade secrets could also result in civil liability under California Civil Code Section 3426, and that willful misappropriation may result in an award against Covenantor for triple the amount of Buyer's damages and Buyer's attorneys fees in collecting such damages.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the termination of Covenantor's employment with Buyer, Covenantor shall endeavor to return relevant materials to Buyer, including notes, data, sketches, drawings, manuals, documents, records, data bases, programs, blueprints, memoranda, specifications, customer lists, financial reports, equipment and all other physical forms of expression incorporating or containing Confidential Information, as reasonably determined by Covenantor. Covenantor acknowledges that the unauthorized taking of any of Buyer's trade secrets is a crime under California Penal Code section 499(c) and is punishable by imprisonment in a state prison or in a county jail for a time not exceeding one year, or by a fine not exceeding five thousand dollars ($5,000), or by both such fine and such imprisonment. Covenantor further acknowledges that such unauthorized taking of Buyer's trade secrets could also result in civil liability under California Civil Code Section 3426, and that willful misappropriation may result in an award against Covenantor for triple the amount of Buyer's damages and Buyer's attorneys fees in collecting such damages.
perturbation.explanation: Modifying 'shall immediately surrender' to 'shall endeavor to return relevant materials...as reasonably determined by Covenantor' introduces ambiguity. The phrase 'relevant materials' and 'as reasonably determined by Covenantor' gives the Covenantor discretion over what to return, potentially allowing them to withhold confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), California Civil Code Section 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: CUTSA aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation. By allowing the Covenantor to decide what is 'relevant' and what is 'reasonably determined,' the modification undermines the protection of trade secrets. It creates ambiguity and potentially allows for the withholding of information that should be protected under the CUTSA, contradicting the purpose and intent of the law.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modification from 'shall immediately surrender' to a discretionary return of 'relevant materials...as reasonably determined by Covenantor' creates a contradiction. CUTSA aims to protect trade secrets, and this change introduces ambiguity allowing the Covenantor to withhold potentially crucial confidential information, undermining the law's intent to prevent misappropriation. While the provided law snippets don't explicitly define 'misappropriation' or the exact requirements for returning confidential information, the Law Explanation highlights that allowing the Covenantor to decide what's 'relevant' contradicts the purpose of trade secret protection, creating a situation where misappropriation could occur more easily, and thus a contradiction under the Ambiguities Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any action, suit, or other proceeding concerning the negotiation, interpretation, validity, performance, or breach of this Agreement, the prevailing party shall recover all of such party's reasonable attorneys' fees, expenses, and costs, not limited to costs of suit, incurred in each and every such action, suit, or other proceeding, including any and all appeals or petitions relating thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any action, suit, or other proceeding concerning the negotiation, interpretation, validity, performance, or breach of this Agreement, the prevailing party may recover some of such party's attorneys' fees, expenses, and costs, not limited to costs of suit, incurred in each and every such action, suit, or other proceeding, including any and all appeals or petitions relating thereto.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall recover all' to 'may recover some' significantly weakens the prevailing party's right to attorney's fees. It introduces uncertainty about whether fees will be awarded and how much, potentially discouraging parties from enforcing their rights under the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021
perturbation.law_explanation: While California Code of Civil Procedure Section 1021 generally states parties bear their own attorney fees unless a statute or agreement provides otherwise, a contract providing for attorney's fees 'to the prevailing party' typically means all reasonable fees. Changing 'shall recover all' to 'may recover some' creates ambiguity and undermines the contractual intent to provide full fee recovery, potentially conflicting with how courts interpret and enforce attorney fee provisions in contracts under California law. This uncertainty could impact the willingness of a party to pursue legal action for breach of contract.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The change from "shall recover all" to "may recover some" introduces ambiguity regarding the recovery of attorney's fees, weakening the prevailing party's right. While CCP 1021 generally allows parties to agree on attorney's fees, the original contract clearly stipulated 'all' reasonable fees. The modified text undermines this clear agreement, potentially leading to disputes and contradicting the intent to provide full fee recovery as typically interpreted by courts, as highlighted in the Law Explanation.
